questions.question: What is the dose-response relationship between team sport participation frequency and resilience?

options.text: Frequency doesn't matter at all
OR 1.20 for 1–3x monthly → OR 1.38 for 1–2x weekly → OR 1.72 for 3+x weekly
Only daily participation shows any benefit
More frequent participation reduces benefits due to fatigue

questions.explanation: The cross-sectional study of 67,281 school students found a clear dose-response pattern: OR 1.20 for participating 1–3 times monthly, increasing to 1.38 for 1–2 times weekly, and 1.72 for 3 or more times weekly for resilience. This shows that even moderate participation (1-3x monthly) provides measurable benefit, but more frequent participation is associated with greater resilience. Consistency matters.

questions.question: What evidence links community belonging from team sport to better self-rated mental health?

options.text: There is no research on community belonging and mental health
People reporting strong community belonging are over 5.7 times more likely to report better self-rated mental health (OR = 5.778)
Community belonging has only weak effects on mental health
Community belonging only matters for social health, not mental health

questions.explanation: People reporting strong community belonging are over 5.7 times more likely to report better self-rated mental health (OR = 5.778, 95% CI 4.879–6.842). This is a massive effect size. Team sport and collective activity foster this sense of community belonging, which is one of the most robust predictors of mental and physical health. Belonging explained 27.4% of variance in wellbeing in research studies.

questions.question: What types of team sports are most accessible for beginners looking for recreational mental health benefits?

options.text: Only high-skill competitive leagues
Recreational soccer, basketball, volleyball, ultimate frisbee, pickleball, softball/baseball leagues — explicitly marketed as beginner-friendly or social
You must start in youth sport to benefit from team sports as an adult
There are no beginner-friendly team sports for adults

questions.explanation: Recreational team sports like soccer, basketball, volleyball, ultimate frisbee, pickleball, and softball/baseball leagues often have divisions explicitly marketed as 'beginner-friendly,' 'social,' or 'recreational.' These environments welcome all skill levels, focus on fun rather than winning, and provide the mental health benefits (connection, belonging, enjoyment) without performance pressure. You don't need prior experience or high skill to benefit.

questions.question: How does team identity formation contribute to mental health benefits?

options.text: Team identity doesn't matter — only physical activity matters
Shared goals and collective challenge create team identity, which provides meaning, purpose, and sense of 'we' rather than just 'I'
Team identity only matters in competitive sport
Individual identity is more important than team identity

questions.explanation: Team identity formation through shared goals, shared experience, and ingroup membership provides meaning and purpose that individual exercise cannot. When you shift from 'I do this activity' to 'I am part of this team,' your sense of self expands to include the group. This provides psychological resources: a sense of 'we' creates belonging, purpose, and meaning. Team identity is one of the core mechanisms through which team sport improves mental health.

questions.question: When should someone NOT rely on recreational team sport alone for mental health support?

options.text: Recreational team sport can always replace professional mental health care
When experiencing significant depression, anxiety, trauma, or other clinical conditions requiring professional assessment and treatment
Never — team sport is more effective than therapy
Only if you've tried it for a full year with no improvement

questions.explanation: Recreational team sport provides mental health BENEFITS as part of a healthy lifestyle, but it's not a substitute for professional treatment when professional treatment is needed. If you experience significant depression, anxiety, trauma, eating disorders, suicidal ideation, or other clinical-level mental health conditions, you need professional assessment and treatment. Team sport can be a valuable COMPLEMENT to therapy and medication — but not a replacement.

title: Team Sports for Mental Health - Quiz
